data.title: Retirees may need $185K for healthcare, Fidelity estimates
data.description:
(NewsNation) — A 65-year-old retiring this year can expect to spend $185,500 on healthcare and medical expenses throughout their golden years.

That’s up 7.5 percent from Fidelity Investments’ estimate of $172,500 a year ago and more than double its inaugural estimate from 2002.

This year’s increase is nearly twice as large as the jump from 2024 to 2025 and reflects rising prices for care, increased utilization and growing costs tied to chronic conditions, according to Fidelity.

The latest estimate comes amid a major demographic transition, with roughly 4 million Americans turning 65 each year through 2027.

Fidelity’s $185,500 projection assumes retirees are enrolled in Medicare Parts A, B and D and includes premiums, copayments and other out-of-pocket costs for medical care and prescription drugs throughout retirement.

More than half of preretirees (54 percent) incorrectly believe Medicare will cover all of their health expenses, the financial institution found.

“Medicare is a critical part of retirement health coverage, but it does not eliminate every health care expense,” Steve Betts, head of Fidelity Health, said in a release.

Importantly, Fidelity’s estimate does not include long-term care costs, which can add up quickly for retirees who eventually need that care.

A recent analysis for the Health and Human Services Department estimated that
56 percent of people turning 65 between 2021 and 2025 will need some form of long-term care during their lifetime.

Consulting firm Milliman estimated earlier this year that 65-year-olds should set aside $135,000 to cover future paid long-term care costs.

Taken together, the estimates serve as a reminder that planning for retirement means preparing not only for everyday living expenses but also for significant medical and long-term care costs.

That financial pressure could also be keeping some Americans in the workforce longer than they intended.

Nearly 1 in 4 U.S. workers, about 23 million adults, say they’re staying in their current job even though they want to leave because they’re afraid of losing their health insurance, according to a new study from the West Health-Gallup Center on Healthcare in America.

data.title: Musk on his involvement in politics: 'I got carried away'
data.description:
Tech mogul Elon Musk reflected on his brief venture into politics in a new interview released Thursday, suggesting he “got carried away.”

“I think I got a little too involved in politics,” the Tesla and SpaceX CEO told The Economist. “I got carried away frankly.”

Musk took an increased interest in politics amid the 2024 elections, pouring more than $250 million into backing President Trump. After Trump won, he announced the billionaire would head up a cost-cutting effort known as the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE).

“The goal with the sort of DOGE stuff was to try to do something about the deficit, which is massive,” Musk said in the interview.

“We need to sort of take a close look at spending and make sure that money is being spent in sensible ways and that it is not wasted or spent on fraudulent activities,” he added. “And I got a lot of flak from this.”

DOGE was highly controversial, leading to sweeping job and funding cuts across the federal government. It officially shut down operations on July 4.

However, the initiative’s efforts had been waning for over a year following Musk’s departure in late May 2025.

The contentious nature of his work at DOGE spilled over onto his companies, particularly Tesla, which saw its stock sink sharply during his brief four-month stint in the Trump administration.

Musk became embroiled in a public dispute with Trump shortly after leaving the White House over the sweeping spending bill, known as the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, that was championed by the president.

The tech mogul threatened to launch a third party, but the effort never got off the ground. He and Trump appear to have mended their rift, with Musk attending a White House dinner last November and joining the president on a trip to China with other tech leaders in May.

data.title: GLP-1 drugs could reduce long-term sick leave: Study
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Popular weight loss drugs could benefit the economy by reducing sick time, according to new research.

An economic study published this month found that GLP-1 medications, which include Ozempic and Wegovy, resulted in employees taking 17 percent less long-term sick leave.

The study, published by the National Bureau of Economic Research, defined “long-term” as absences of more than 30 days related to illness and used Danish data to examine the connection between the drugs and absences.

Originally developed as diabetes drugs, GLP-1s have soared in popularity when used for weight loss. Ongoing research suggests the medications could have other positive impacts, including reduced risks of cardiovascular disease and some cancers.

The reduced use of sick time translated to savings of up to 1.5 percent of a worker’s salary.

Denmark’s sick leave policies are more generous than the U.S., with employers required to pay workers a full salary for illness-related absences up to 30 days and the government covering long-term leave past the month mark.

data.title: Loomer meets Zelensky after turnaround on war
data.description:
Far-right influencer Laura Loomer met with Ukraine President Volodymyr Zelensky on Thursday after publicly reversing her long-held and outspoken opposition to backing Ukraine in its war with Russia, expressing support for the country in a visit to Kyiv.

Loomer, who is a loyal ally of President Trump, has frequently criticized U.S. military aid to Ukraine amid its years-long war with Russia, but said experiencing air raid sirens firsthand while in Ukraine changed her perspective.

She apologized for her previous comments about Ukraine two days after Russia launched a deadly attack on Kyiv that killed 6 people.

“Just experienced my first air raid in Ukraine,” she wrote in a post on the social platform X earlier this week. “I feel like such an a—— for minimizing the struggle of Ukrainians for the last 5 years. I often said I don’t care. Looking back, that wasn’t very nice of me to say.”

Loomer sat down with Zelenksy for an interview on her Rumble show, “Loomer Unleashed,” marking a striking reversal for the conservative influencer who has previously accused the Ukrainian government of supporting jihadists and of being “full of Nazi apologists.”

In a clip released from the interview, Loomer asked Zelensky about his contentious meeting with Trump in the Oval Office earlier this year, and the relationship between the two leaders.

Zelensky told Loomer that a brief meeting with Trump at the Vatican helped repair the relationship.

“It was a historic moment, because we changed the relations during 15, 20 minutes,” he said.

Trump appeared supportive of Loomer’s meeting with Zelensky, posting clips of the interview and writing, “Very good!!!” on social media.

Zelensky’s office praised Loomer’s trip to Kyiv and apologies for her previous comments following her meeting with Ukrainian officials, including First Deputy Head of the Office of the President Sergiy Kyslytsya.

“It takes courage to come to Ukraine and not be deterred by Russian propaganda or air raid sirens,” Zelensky’s office wrote on X. “Thank you, Laura.”

data.title: DOJ sues Milwaukee over mask ban for federal agents
data.description:
The Trump administration sued Milwaukee on Thursday over the city’s ban on law enforcement wearing face coverings while on duty.

The Department of Justice (DOJ) argued the measure is unconstitutional when used against federal agents, pointing to the administration’s recent wins in similar legal challenges it launched against California, Virginia and Philadelphia.

“This Court should join the growing number of others that are putting an end to this type of blatant unconstitutional regulation of the Federal Government,” the new lawsuit reads.

This spring, Milwaukee joined a growing number of localities that have passed restrictions on how law enforcement, including federal agents, can operate. It’s part of pushback by local Democratic officials against President Trump’s sweeping immigration crackdown.

The city’s ordinance bans law enforcement officers from wearing a face covering “while interacting with the public in the performance of the officer’s duties.” Agents must also visibly display a badge or alternatively provide their affiliation and information verbally upon request.

Exceptions exist for undercover operations, cold weather and officers who need to wear special gear, but violations carry fines of up to $10,000.

The city has stood firm as DOJ has stepped up its scrutiny, court documents show.

City Attorney Evan Goyke insisted in a July 17 letter that the ordinance was “legal and enforceable.”

“Neither the preliminary court rulings in other jurisdictions nor your letter convince us to change that opinion,” Goyke wrote to the department. “We will not advise our clients to exempt federal law enforcement officers from the Ordinance and we will prosecute properly cited offenders.”

The administration wants a judge to rule that the ban unlawfully regulates the federal government in violation of the Constitution’s Supremacy Clause.

“Law enforcement historically has been a dangerous job but now is even more so,” the suit states.

“Protecting the personal identities of federal officers and by extension their families is necessary in part due to the increasing threats of targeted harassment and retaliation against federal officers and agents for simply doing their jobs,” it reads.

data.title: The drug policy debate's common ground: better treatment and recovery
data.description:
Few public policy debates are as polarized as drug policy, which is often framed around two competing visions — prohibition and decriminalization. Both camps are passionate about their positions, and both make valid arguments. But they frequently overlook the same prerequisite for success: a system capable of meeting the country’s growing demand for addiction treatment and recovery services. 

Prohibitionists point to the extraordinary dangers posed by today’s drugs, which are often far more lethal and addictive than in the past, and suggest they must be outlawed to protect public health and safety. They have a point. Synthetic opioids like fentanyl can be more than 50 times as potent as heroin and were involved in more than 55,000 overdose deaths in the last 12 months, according to Centers for Disease Control and Prevention provisional data. These drugs are deadly, and demand strict controls. 

Drug reformers note the failure of the so-called “war on drugs” to curb drug trafficking, use and overdose deaths, and support a new approach grounded in “evidence, health, equity, and human rights.” They have a point. After over 50 years of prohibition, drugs are cheaper, stronger and easier to get than ever, despite massive investments in interdiction and enforcement. Now is the time to change course and treat illicit drugs primarily as a public health issue.  

Whatever one’s preferred approach, both prohibition and reform depend on the availability of effective treatment and recovery services. 

Take prohibition. History shows that increased regulation has at times resulted in unintended consequences. In 2005, for example, the U.S. government introduced strict controls on the sale of ephedrine and pseudoephedrine to disrupt domestic methamphetamine production. The crackdown was wildly successful but had the disastrous effect of shifting production to Mexico, where it quickly grew to an unprecedented scale and helped enrich powerful drug trafficking organizations. 

More recently, as doctors cut back on prescribing opioid painkillers like OxyContin, many patients turned to heroin and fentanyl to stave off withdrawal. In both cases, restricting supply without simultaneously increasing access to treatment simply displaced demand rather than reducing it. 

When it comes to decriminalization, Oregon’s Measure 110, which removed criminal penalties for the possession of small amounts of illicit drugs such as methamphetamine, heroin, and cocaine, failed in large part due to insufficient treatment programs or effective pathways to care.

Oregon’s law replaced criminal penalties with a $100 fine for drug possession (which would be waived if individuals called a toll-free number for a health assessment). More than 95 percent of people ignored the ticket, and fewer than 1 percent completed the health assessment. Oregon wasn’t ready to support the influx of users seeking treatment, even if they wanted it.  

Both approaches ultimately run into the same obstacle: an inadequate treatment infrastructure. While establishing a new Department of Treatment and Recovery — as I have previously suggested — may be a bridge too far, there are other more immediate and practical ways to strengthen the nation’s treatment and recovery system. It starts with two fundamentals: people and places. 

First, there is a critical shortage of nearly every occupation in the drug treatment and recovery field, from addiction counselors to social workers, case managers, and healthcare professionals. As of 2025, more than 40 percent of the U.S. population lived in mental health professional shortage areas, where demand far exceeds available providers. These shortages are even more acute in rural counties and carceral settings, where substance use and overdose death rates are disproportionately high. 

Investing in the behavioral health workforce is an urgent national priority as millions of Americans continue to require long-term support. The U.S. must help promote careers in treatment and recovery through more competitive pay, streamlined education and licensing pathways, stable funding and stronger peer support systems that reduce burnout and turnover.    

The United States also faces a severe shortage of behavioral health and substance use treatment facilities. Inpatient detox beds, residential treatment centers, and outpatient clinics remain in short supply nationwide. Nearly 80 percent of U.S. counties lack an opioid treatment program — the only place to legally obtain methadone, one of three Food and Drug Administration-approved medications used to treat opioid use disorder. The state of Wyoming has none. 

In addition to bolstering human capital, the United States must rapidly scale the physical infrastructure needed to deliver quality care. That means increasing treatment capacity, extending services into underserved communities and creating safe, accessible settings where people can receive immediate care and begin long-term recovery. 

These investments represent the floor — not the ceiling — needed to transform the U.S. drug treatment and recovery system. For far too long, U.S. policymakers, prohibitionists and reformers alike, have neglected vital parts of the continuum of care, leaving many Americans with substance use disorder out in the cold. No matter which way you lean, expanding these services is essential to advancing drug policy and saving lives. 

Building that system is no longer optional — it is the foundation upon which effective drug policy must rest. 

Jim Crotty is the former deputy chief of staff for the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration. He is an adjunct professor at American University’s School of Public Affairs, and a member of the Advisory Board for United Against Fentanyl and End Overdose.

data.title: The US owes the people of Hong Kong an explanation
data.description:
Six years ago, the U.S. drew a line on Hong Kong.  

After Beijing imposed a sweeping national security law on the city, criminalizing dissent and effectively ending the “one country, two systems” framework that had defined Hong Kong since the 1997 handover, President Trump signed Executive Order 13936. It declared a national emergency, stripped Hong Kong of the preferential trade and export-control treatment it had long enjoyed as a distinct customs territory, and opened the door to sanctions on officials responsible for dismantling the city’s freedoms. 

That line just quietly disappeared.

On July 14, the national emergency underlying Trump’s order lapsed. There was no announcement from the White House, no policy speech explaining the reasoning, just silence. The administration simply declined to file the routine renewal notice it had filed every year since 2020.  

It was Beijing, not Washington, that told the world first. China’s Ministry of Commerce announced the news on July 17, framing it as the fulfillment of commitments the U.S. reportedly made during trade talks in Madrid last September. The Treasury Department later confirmed the decision, but has offered no public explanation of what was agreed to or why. 

The American public, and the people of Hong Kong, deserve that explanation.  

The practical stakes are real. Sanctions relief now extends to nine officials directly responsible for the crackdown on Hong Kong, including Justice Secretary Paul Lam, National Security Department Chief Sonny Au, and former police commissioners Raymond Siu and Stephen Lo. These individuals have spent years enforcing a legal apparatus that has jailed pro-democracy activists, shuttered independent newsrooms and, as recently as this spring, allowed authorities to freeze the assets of imprisoned publisher and British citizen Jimmy Lai. The lapse may also pave the way for Hong Kong to regain the preferential trade status and export-control exemptions the order revoked in 2020.

None of this reflects an improvement on the ground. Just this year, Hong Kong officials implemented new national-security offenses criminalizing refusal to hand over passwords to police, sweeping new powers letting the city’s leader certify any case as a national-security matter beyond judicial review, and continued asset seizures reaching dissidents abroad.  

Judging by the very standard the order itself set in 2020 — an “unusual and extraordinary threat“ to Hong Kong’s autonomy — nothing has changed except Washington’s willingness to say so. 

There is a human cost that rarely makes it into a Treasury statement. Thousands of Hong Kongers have come to the United States in recent years, many fleeing the very crackdown the national emergency was designed to answer. They came here believing that America, its laws and its willingness to stand up to Beijing was a genuine refuge from a government that had already shown it would reach across borders to silence its critics. Hong Kong authorities have placed bounties on pro-democracy activists living overseas, and have launched transnational repression campaigns reaching diaspora communities from Melbourne to London to right here in the U.S.  

For many of these families, Washington’s silence sends a signal that the country they trusted to protect them may no longer be willing to do so.

This was not a reassessment prompted by improvements in Hong Kong. To all indications, it was a trade concession, negotiated behind closed doors, confirmed not by the administration that made it but by the government that benefited from it. The non-renewal of the national emergency does not just fail to hold Beijing accountable — it hands the Chinese Communist Party a win it has not earned.  

Congress and the public deserve real answers. What, specifically, did the United States receive from Beijing in exchange? Were human rights conditions ever part of that negotiation, or was Hong Kong simply a chip on a larger table involving tariffs and technology? 

Congress does not have to accept silence as an answer. It has the tools to find out: hearings, document requests, and the oversight authority it already holds over U.S.-China policy. Lawmakers from both parties supported the Hong Kong Human Rights and Democracy Act and the Hong Kong Autonomy Act by overwhelming bipartisan margins because they understood that human rights and trade policy should not be interchangeable. That principle is worth defending now, not just in 2020. 

The administration must reconsider this decision and ensure that sanctions on the Hong Kong officials responsible for dismantling the city’s judicial independence and civil liberties remain firmly in place.  

The people of Hong Kong, the judges forced from the bench, the journalists who have fled or been jailed, activists like Jimmy Lai still sitting in prison, and the families who crossed an ocean believing in the promise of American freedom, did not choose this moment of American retreat. They deserve better than to be a bargaining chip in a trade deal negotiated on their behalf but without their voice.

At minimum, they deserve an honest explanation. 

Megan Khoo is policy director at Hong Kong Watch.

data.title: Explosions heard near US base in Iraq after Pentagon finishes latest strikes against Iran
data.description:
Explosive-laden drones targeted a military base in northern Iraq that hosts American forces on Friday, hours after the U.S. military launched its 13th consecutive night of strikes against Iran.

Kurdish security officials said coalition forces shot down five drones over Erbil, Iraq — where troops were stationed at the Erbil International Airport.

A journalist for The Associated Press reported hearing at least seven explosions and seeing four plumes of black smoke near the city’s airport. Officials confirmed that “the incident caused no casualties or damage.”

The strikes came hours after the U.S. military, which has at least 2,500 troops stationed in Iraq, confirmed it completed another night of strikes against Iranian military sites it says were used to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

“This is the 13th consecutive night of strikes aimed to hold Iran accountable and diminish threats from the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps to commercial shipping,” a Pentagon official announced Thursday.

Warning sirens were also heard in Bahrain, which hosts the U.S. Navy’s Fifth Fleet. Bahrain’s Interior Ministry confirmed the sirens in a series of warnings posted to social platform X.

Bahrain’s military confirmed that it intercepted several attacks by Iran in a post on social media.

“The air defenses of the Bahrain Defense Force intercepted and destroyed a number of Iranian aerial aggressions,” the military said in a statement.

Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebels also claimed responsibility for attacks against two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, prompting the U.S. military to take action amid escalating tensions over commercial and oil shipping routes.

President Trump on Thursday warned the Houthis would face “major military punishment” if attacks on commercial vessels continue. He also threatened to use frozen Iranian assets held by the U.S. to pay for damage to ships targeted during the conflict.

“Any and all damages done to Ships, Cargo, or anything related thereto, will be paid for by Iranian Money that the United States has in its possession, and controls,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: Live updates: Trump's tariffs set to kick in; White House Correspondents' dinner re-do
data.description:
President Trump’s 10 percent tariffs are set to expire on Friday, but U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced new levies on Thursday to replace them.

The fresh import taxes –– ranging from 10 percent to 12.5 percent –– will hit 60 countries, accounting for 99 percent of U.S. imports. They are pegged to the elimination of forced labor. International trading partners ripped the new tariffs on Friday.

U.S. forces have launched strikes against Iran for nearly two weeks straight, and Republican defense hawks are urging Trump to unleash major combat operations. 

Trump will speak Friday evening at the rescheduled White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA) dinner. The WHCA said earlier this week it will honor a Secret Service agent who was pivotal in stopping a gunman who tried to assassinate Trump during the annual dinner this spring.

The House is now on a five-week recess, while the Senate is set to return Monday. And tension is rising between Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and the White House as the upper chamber struggles to pass Trump’s key priority: the SAVE America Act.

Follow live below.

data.title: Deadline to file claim in $2.5B settlement over Amazon Prime coming soon
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Those who were impacted by the alleged “deceptive methods” Amazon used for Prime subscriptions have just days to file a claim to receive part of a $2.5 billion settlement.

Amazon settled with the Federal Trade Commission last year after the company was accused of using “deceptive methods” to register millions of customers for paid Prime subscriptions, then making it difficult for those customers to cancel. Amazon agreed to pay a $2.5 billion settlement ($1.5 billion will fund customer refunds) and to “cease unlawful enrollment and cancellation practices for Prime.”

Amazon, which did not admit wrongdoing, previously said that while it has “always followed the law … this settlement allows us to move forward and focus on innovating for customers.”

Automatic refunds were sent out to some impacted customers already, and earlier this year, the claim process opened for other eligible Prime customers. That process closes in just a few days.

Who qualifies to file a claim?

You have to be a U.S. Prime member who signed up for Prime between June 23, 2019, and June 23, 2025. You also had to have “unintentionally” signed up with Amazon’s “challenged enrollment flow” or tried and failed to cancel your subscription. Further, you had to have used more than three Prime benefits, like Prime Music or Prime Video, but fewer than 10 between June 23, 2019, and June 23, 2025.

You don’t need to verify that you meet all three conditions. Instead, the FTC said Amazon would notify qualifying customers who have not already received automatic refunds. Those claim notices were delivered to mailboxes or emails and postmarked in late January.

You will need that notice to file a claim.

How do I file a claim?

You can do so online via the settlement website. You’ll need to enter the claim ID and PIN listed on your notice. Officials warned that the process does not require you to pay a fee or enter your Social Security number or Amazon login.

There is an option to file a claim without an ID or PIN.

You will be asked to enter your address, email, and phone number or email associated with the Amazon account. The form will then ask you multiple questions to confirm you are not “a bot or person attempting to file multiple claims on behalf of other purchasers.” Then you will be asked how you would like to receive your payment and to electronically sign the form before submitting.

How much will I receive, and when is the deadline?

You have until Monday, July 27, to submit the online claim form, according to the settlement administrators.

Payments, which are capped at $51 but could be less than $1, are expected to be disbursed later this year.

There is no opt-out option, but you can choose not to file a claim, officials said.
